tools.name: save-memory
tools.description: Saves a new memory to the database. Use this to persist knowledge, facts, preferences, or any information that should be remembered across conversations.

title.description: Short descriptive title for the memory.

content.description: The content of the memory. Can be a fact, preference, instruction, or any knowledge worth persisting.

tools.name: query-memories
tools.description: Search for stored memories using full-text search. Returns memories matching the query terms. Use this to recall previously saved knowledge, facts, or preferences.

text.description: Full-text search query. Omit to list all memories.

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return.

tools.name: delete-memory
tools.description: Deletes a memory from the database. Use this to remove outdated or incorrect memories.

memory.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You have the ability to save and recall memories.
  Memories are persistent knowledge units stored in the database.
  Use memories to remember facts, user preferences, decisions, and any knowledge that should persist across conversations.
  
  When the user asks you to remember something, save it as a memory with a descriptive title.
  When the user asks about something you might have stored, query your memories first.
  You can also delete outdated or incorrect memories.
</blueprint>
Remember that my favorite programming language is TypeScript.

response.delta: user wants me to remember their favorite programming language. I'll save this as a memory.

content.text: Let me compile a great list of hotel recommendations across different categories and price ranges, then save it to memory.

Here are some excellent hotel recommendations for your LA trip! I've organized them by neighborhood and budget:

---

### 🌟 **Luxury / Splurge**

1. **Hotel Bel-Air** —
A gorgeous Dorchester Collection property that brings Hollywood regency into a new era, set on a 12-acre oasis hidden in the winding canyons of Bel-Air.
Rates: ~$800–$1,200/night.
2. **The Peninsula Beverly Hills** —
Operates like a five-star hotel should — front desk escorts guests to rooms, delivers handwritten welcome notes, and rooms start at 750 sq ft, double the size of most luxury hotels in the city.
Expect $800–$1,200/night, but the service and room size justify the premium.
3. **The Beverly Hills Hotel** —
Still charges $1,145/night for standard rooms with its iconic banana-leaf wallpaper.
Has a red carpet running up its entrance steps — a quintessential Los Angeles icon.
4. **The Maybourne Beverly Hills** —
From the group behind Claridge's and The Connaught in London, with 205 redesigned guest rooms featuring mid-century furniture and collectible artworks.

### 🏖️ **Beach / Santa Monica**

5. **Casa del Mar** —
Runs $692–$1,606/night for direct beach access that only two LA hotels can claim.
6. **Santa Monica Proper Hotel** —
A blend of contemporary design and coastal elegance that captures the laid-back essence of Santa Monica with a touch of sophistication.

### 🏙️ **Downtown LA / Mid-Range**

7. **Hotel Figueroa** —
One of the longest-standing hotels in DTLA, dazzling thanks to a two-year restoration inspired by its original 1926 Spanish Colonial splendor, with 268 beautifully appointed rooms.
Rates run $200–$400/night — one of the better values in central LA.
8. **Downtown LA Proper Hotel** —
In DTLA's historic core with two on-site restaurants helmed by James Beard award-winning chefs, plus proximity to MOCA, The Broad, and Walt Disney Concert Hall.
9. **Ritz-Carlton Downtown LA** —
Commands some of the best panoramic views of the city, occupying the top four floors of a striking glass tower, within strolling distance of the Crypto.com Arena and Grammy Museum.

### 🎸 **West Hollywood / Boutique**

10. **PUBLIC West Hollywood** *(New in 2026!)* —
A brand-new opening by Ian Schrager in the former Standard Hotel building.
Features a 16,000-sq-ft open-air rooftop terrace with 360-degree views of Los Angeles.
11. **Hotel Covell** —
With only nine suites, it's one of the most intimate boutique hotels in East Hollywood — like staying in your dream apartment. ~$289/night.

---

### 💡 **Quick Tips**
-

The trick is matching the neighborhood to your purpose: Beverly Hills for shopping/meetings, Santa Monica for beach access, Downtown for arts/culture, and West Hollywood for restaurants and nightlife.
Hotel prices in LA are usually on the higher side, but you can find deals by booking early.

---

tools.name: save-memory
tools.description: Saves a new memory to the database. Use this to persist knowledge, facts, preferences, or any information that should be remembered across conversations.

title.description: Short descriptive title for the memory.

content.description: The content of the memory. Can be a fact, preference, instruction, or any knowledge worth persisting.

tools.name: query-memories
tools.description: Search for stored memories using full-text search. Returns memories matching the query terms. Use this to recall previously saved knowledge, facts, or preferences.

text.description: Full-text search query. Omit to list all memories.

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return.

tools.name: delete-memory
tools.description: Deletes a memory from the database. Use this to remove outdated or incorrect memories.

memory.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You have the ability to save and recall memories.
  Memories are persistent knowledge units stored in the database.
  Use memories to remember facts, user preferences, decisions, and any knowledge that should persist across conversations.
  
  When the user asks you to remember something, save it as a memory with a descriptive title.
  When the user asks about something you might have stored, query your memories first.
  You can also delete outdated or incorrect memories.
</blueprint>
Remember that my favorite programming language is TypeScript.

content.text: The user wants me to remember their favorite programming language. I'll save this as a memory.

params.title: Favorite programming language
params.content: The user's favorite programming language is TypeScript.

value.content: The user's favorite programming language is TypeScript.
value.title: Favorite programming language

response.delta: it! I've saved that your favorite programming language is TypeScript. I'll remember this for future conversations! 🎉

content.text: Let me compile a great list of hotel recommendations across different categories and price ranges, then save it to memory.

Here are some excellent hotel recommendations for your LA trip! I've organized them by neighborhood and budget:

---

### 🌟 **Luxury / Splurge**

1. **Hotel Bel-Air** —
A gorgeous Dorchester Collection property that brings Hollywood regency into a new era, set on a 12-acre oasis hidden in the winding canyons of Bel-Air.
Rates: ~$800–$1,200/night.
2. **The Peninsula Beverly Hills** —
Operates like a five-star hotel should — front desk escorts guests to rooms, delivers handwritten welcome notes, and rooms start at 750 sq ft, double the size of most luxury hotels in the city.
Expect $800–$1,200/night, but the service and room size justify the premium.
3. **The Beverly Hills Hotel** —
Still charges $1,145/night for standard rooms with its iconic banana-leaf wallpaper.
Has a red carpet running up its entrance steps — a quintessential Los Angeles icon.
4. **The Maybourne Beverly Hills** —
From the group behind Claridge's and The Connaught in London, with 205 redesigned guest rooms featuring mid-century furniture and collectible artworks.

### 🏖️ **Beach / Santa Monica**

5. **Casa del Mar** —
Runs $692–$1,606/night for direct beach access that only two LA hotels can claim.
6. **Santa Monica Proper Hotel** —
A blend of contemporary design and coastal elegance that captures the laid-back essence of Santa Monica with a touch of sophistication.

### 🏙️ **Downtown LA / Mid-Range**

7. **Hotel Figueroa** —
One of the longest-standing hotels in DTLA, dazzling thanks to a two-year restoration inspired by its original 1926 Spanish Colonial splendor, with 268 beautifully appointed rooms.
Rates run $200–$400/night — one of the better values in central LA.
8. **Downtown LA Proper Hotel** —
In DTLA's historic core with two on-site restaurants helmed by James Beard award-winning chefs, plus proximity to MOCA, The Broad, and Walt Disney Concert Hall.
9. **Ritz-Carlton Downtown LA** —
Commands some of the best panoramic views of the city, occupying the top four floors of a striking glass tower, within strolling distance of the Crypto.com Arena and Grammy Museum.

### 🎸 **West Hollywood / Boutique**

10. **PUBLIC West Hollywood** *(New in 2026!)* —
A brand-new opening by Ian Schrager in the former Standard Hotel building.
Features a 16,000-sq-ft open-air rooftop terrace with 360-degree views of Los Angeles.
11. **Hotel Covell** —
With only nine suites, it's one of the most intimate boutique hotels in East Hollywood — like staying in your dream apartment. ~$289/night.

---

### 💡 **Quick Tips**
-

The trick is matching the neighborhood to your purpose: Beverly Hills for shopping/meetings, Santa Monica for beach access, Downtown for arts/culture, and West Hollywood for restaurants and nightlife.
Hotel prices in LA are usually on the higher side, but you can find deals by booking early.

---

Now let me save these recommendations for you!

10. PUBLIC West Hollywood — New 2026 opening by Ian Schrager, 16,000 sq ft rooftop.
11. Hotel Covell — Only 9 suites, intimate boutique, ~$289/night.

TIPS: Match neighborhood to purpose. Beverly Hills = shopping/meetings. Santa Monica = beach. DTLA = arts/culture. WeHo = restaurants/nightlife. Book early for better rates.

value.content:
Hotel recommendations for upcoming LA trip:

LUXURY:
1. Hotel Bel-Air — Dorchester Collection, 12-acre oasis in Bel-Air canyons. $800-$1,200/night.
2. The Peninsula Beverly Hills — Top-tier service, 750 sq ft rooms. $800-$1,200/night.
3. The Beverly Hills Hotel — Iconic, banana-leaf wallpaper. ~$1,145/night.
4. The Maybourne Beverly Hills — From Claridge's group, mid-century design. 205 rooms.

BEACH / SANTA MONICA:
5. Casa del Mar — Direct beach access. $692-$1,606/night.
6. Santa Monica Proper Hotel — Contemporary coastal design, laid-back vibe.

DOWNTOWN LA / MID-RANGE:
7. Hotel Figueroa — Historic 1926 Spanish Colonial, great pool. $200-$400/night.
8. Downtown LA Proper Hotel — James Beard chefs on-site, near MOCA & The Broad.
9. Ritz-Carlton Downtown LA — Top-floor panoramic views, near Crypto.com Arena.

WEST HOLLYWOOD / BOUTIQUE:
10. PUBLIC West Hollywood — New 2026 opening by Ian Schrager, 16,000 sq ft rooftop.
11. Hotel Covell — Only 9 suites, intimate boutique, ~$289/night.

TIPS: Match neighborhood to purpose. Beverly Hills = shopping/meetings. Santa Monica = beach. DTLA = arts/culture. WeHo = restaurants/nightlife. Book early for better rates.
